div.h3: _IOW / _IOR / _IO bit-field layout · computed hex values · macro extraction

g.text: How strace decodes ioctls without driver source
Macro names use the **userspace perspective** (`_IOW` = user writes).
`_IOC_DIR()` bits use the **kernel perspective** (`_IOC_WRITE` = kernel reads from user).
These are *opposite* — the #1 source of `access_ok()` validation bugs.

text.tspan: Macro names use the **userspace perspective** (`_IOW` = user writes).
`_IOC_DIR()` bits use the **kernel perspective** (`_IOC_WRITE` = kernel reads from user).
These are *opposite* — the #1 source of `access_ok()` validation bugs.
No argument. arg is undefined — never dereference.
Kernel writes struct mydevice_status to userspace.
Size=40 encodes struct layout for strace decoding.
_IOC_WRITE means kernel reads from user ptr.
_IOC_READ means kernel writes to user ptr.
Returns sizeof(arg_type) baked at compile time.
Also used by strace to know how many bytes to display.

g.text: 1. strace intercepts ioctl(fd, cmd, arg) syscall

text.tspan: → knows exactly how many bytes arg points to
No driver header needed — size is in the number itself
→ knows if it should capture before-call (write) or after-call (read) snapshot
→ looks up magic in its compiled-in ioctl table
Unknown magic → printed as raw hex (your 'k' driver)
It decoded size=40 and dir=READ from the cmd bits alone

g.text: A kernel driver with zero userspace headers can be partially decoded
by any tool that understands the `_IOC_*` bit layout.
This is the same principle as TCP port numbers encoding protocol,
or Ethernet ethertypes encoding frame type — structured integers
